text:
Arndt Weinrich | Teaching










Last Update: May 12

With Canvas at EUR currentyl not available, we will use this page to share some important information on the course, including the slides and other materials. Please check this page regularly for updates and announcements.




Osiris/Sqill contains important information on Accounting, Analysis and Governance in Complex Deals (BMME303). Please read all information carefully! Here’s a sneak preview of the course description:
This course integrates accounting, financial analysis, and governance, providing students with advanced skills in understanding, assessing, and executing a variety of different transaction types, such as M&A, reorganizations, and other complex deals. Since such transactions often substantially transform businesses, understanding how to analyze and navigate these disruptions is essential for any professional involved in them.




Understand how (financial) information creates and resolves conflicts between business leaders, oversight bodies, and other stakeholders throughout transactions.
Critically assess complex multi-type and multi-source information in transaction-related valuations and decisions.
Analyze the complex regulatory environment, including disclosure mandates, surrounding a variety of transaction types.
Apply regulatory risk assessment frameworks, including commercial and tax due diligence, to transactions.
Reflect on competencies in accounting, analysis, and governance acquired in other courses throughout the MSc programme to navigate complex deals.




Arndt Weinrich | Assistant Professor of Accounting | weinrich@rsm.nl | RSM website



The course consists of 11 sessions that combine elements of workshops and lectures.
I expect interactive discussions to form the basis of our sessions, requiring students to be prepared, to reflect on previous input, and to actively engage with each other. Therefore, no livestreams or recordings are offered.



Mandatory attendance applies to guest lectures. These sessions are a core component of the course and provide essential content that cannot be replaced through alternative assignments or readings. Students who miss a guest lecture will be ineligible to participate in the corresponding weekly assignment (see assessment element “Assessment - Assignment”), resulting in a score of 0% for that week’s assignment. Note that (i) assignments in total account for 20% of the final grade and (ii) students must pass all assessment elements to pass the course. Absences from guest lectures will thus directly impact your overall performance.





Tentative sessions for Guest Lectures with Mandatory Attendance

May 26, McKinsey & Company, Valuation

June 16, Boston Consulting Group, Synergies




Check timetables.eur.nl for an overview of appointments and room/lecture hall allocations




“80% Element” → Final Exam (closed book) using Chromebook and Ans software
“20% Element” → Assignments (think of weekly quizzes, open questions, and other deliverables, such as case solutions) throughout the course (may include peer feedback)
Note that you must pass all assessment elements in order to pass the course. No carry-overs!
Expectations? Check the assessment rubric for detailed descriptions of expected performance at each level (Beginner, Developing, Accomplished, Exemplary) for every course learning goal





Canvas is currently unavailable at EUR \(\rightarrow\) see this live update blog by EUR IT and follow in-class announcements!
Week One Quiz \(\rightarrow\) start/deadline will likely be postponed until either (i) access to Canvas is restored or (ii) a secure alternative emerges. In any case, I will communicate before sending something online: monitor your EUR inboxes, including MS Teams!




You can download the slides as HTML and PDF. Note that slides in PDF format often lose some features (e.g., embedded tabs, videos, etc.) and are thus not the preferred format.

Slides: Kickoff HTML and PDF
Slides: Transactions HTML and PDF & FAQ on Reading Organizational Charts
Slides: Governance HTML and PDF








While there is no single literature source for the course, the following books are used as key reference materials:

DePamphilis (2025) covers a wide range of topics (2019 version electronically available at EUR)
Koller, Goedhart, and Wessels (2025) is a classic in the field of valuation (2020/7th version electronically available at EUR)

Additionally, academic articles are used to illustrate the topics covered in the course and are exam-relevant (see slides)

Reference List


DePamphilis, Donald M. 2025. Mergers, Acquisitions, and Other Restructuring Activities. Academic Press.


Koller, Tim, Marc Goedhart, and David Wessels. 2025. Valuation. John Wiley & Sons.
